plainMeaning: The Arihantas — the awakened victors — are my one blessing and my only god, and by praising them I let go of my sins.
recitation: arahintā maṅgalaṁ mujjha, arihantā mujjha devayā; arihantā kittiyaṁ tāṇaṁ, vosirāmi tti pāvagaṁ.

wordByWord.gloss: the Arihantas (nominative plural) — the embodied omniscient victors
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Skt. arihanta / arhat (ari 'enemy' + √han 'to slay' — slayer of the inner foes, the passions)
wordByWord.notes: Pāda 1 prints the variant spelling 'arahintā'; the other three occurrences read 'arihantā'. Both are preserved verbatim per the book's orthography.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Skt. maṅgala; nominative/accusative singular neuter
Prakrit genitive-dative singular < Skt. mama / mahyam (Sheth, Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo s.v. 'majjha'; Pischel §415–420)
Prakrit < Skt. arihanta; second of three anaphoric repetitions in the verse
Prakrit < Skt. devatā; the worthy object of refuge, not a worldly favour-granting god
Prakrit < Skt. arihanta; third repetition, opening the praise-line
Prakrit past participle < Skt. kīrtita, from √kṛt 'to proclaim, praise' (kīrtana)
Prakrit genitive plural < Skt. teṣāṁ (Sheth s.v. 'tāṇa/teṇaṁ'; Pischel §423)
Prakrit 1st person sg. present < Skt. vyutsṛjāmi (vi + ut + √sṛj 'to release, let go')
Prakrit < Skt. iti; closes the resolve as a declared formula
Prakrit < Skt. pāpaka (pāpa + -ka); accusative singular, the object cast off

literalTranslation: The Arihantas are my blessing; the Arihantas are my deity; the Arihantas — their glory is proclaimed; thus I cast off sin.
idiomaticTranslation: The awakened Victors are my every blessing; the Victors alone are the god I hold; I proclaim their glory aloud — and so, declaring it, I let my sin fall away.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: The opening verse fastens the whole four-fold prayer to the first and most accessible of the supreme beings: the Arihanta, the omniscient victor who, though still embodied, remains in the world to teach. Note the deliberate anaphora — 'arihantā' is sounded three times, once in each declarative movement (blessing, deity, the-praised-one). This is not redundancy but a meditative fixing: the reciter does not merely think of the Arihanta but repeatedly re-enthrones him at each station of the formula, so the name itself becomes the rhythm of the verse. A doctrinal question naturally arises — why does the descent begin with the Arihanta rather than the higher, wholly-liberated Siddha who follows in verse two? Because the Arihanta is the gateway of knowing: it is the embodied, speaking victor through whom the very existence of the Siddha-state is revealed, so devotion rightly starts with the teacher one can hear before rising to the silent perfection one can only contemplate. The closing line then performs its quiet pivot: having proclaimed 'their glory' (kittiyaṁ tāṇaṁ), the reciter at once declares 'vosirāmi tti pāvagaṁ' — and so I cast off sin. Praise and renunciation are bound in a single breath; the act of extolling the passion-free victor is itself the act by which one's own pāpa is released. The verse thus models, in miniature, the movement the next three verses will repeat through Siddha, Ācārya and Upādhyāya.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: To name the Arihanta as one's 'devayā' is a precise Jain claim: true divinity is not a favour-dispensing world-god but the vītarāga — the one wholly free of attachment and aversion, who has destroyed the four obscuring (ghātī) karmas and attained kevala-jñāna while yet embodied (the sayogī-kevalī). Taking such a being as deity reorients devotion from petition toward emulation: one venerates the qualities one means to make one's own. The verse's logic — that proclaiming this victor's glory is what sheds sin — rests on the Jain understanding that bhāva (inner disposition) toward the supremely passion-free purifies the soul of accreted pāpa; kīrtana enacted as vyutsarga is devotion turned into karmic cleansing.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this verse, the practitioner makes a daily act of allegiance: choosing the awakened ideal — not fortune, fear, or worldly powers — as the single source of auspiciousness worth turning to, and letting that choice itself be the moment of releasing wrongdoing.

notes: The book prints 'અરહિંતા' (arahintā) in pāda 1 against 'અરિહંતા' (arihantā) in the three later occurrences; both are kept verbatim and are not variant readings of sense, only of spelling. Following the preface, 'kittiyaṁ tāṇaṁ' is read as 'their glory is proclaimed' (kīrtitaṁ teṣāṁ), treating kittiyaṁ as Skt. kīrtita and tāṇaṁ as the genitive plural teṣāṁ; an alternative parse reads kittiyaṁ as 'declared' with tāṇaṁ otherwise construed — flagged as a minor ambiguity, with the preface's reading followed as authoritative. 'mujjha' = Skt. mama/mahyam ('my/to me'); 'tti' = quotative iti. The following verse repeats the identical frame substituting the Siddha for the Arihanta.
